title: Economic Bulletin Issue 5, 2025
summary: The ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged at its July 2025 meeting, with inflation at the 2% medium-term target. The euro area economy grew more strongly than expected in Q1 2025, partly due to frontloaded exports ahead of expected tariff hikes, supported by resilient consumption and investment. The Governing Council emphasized data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach and noted exceptional uncertainty from trade disputes.
key_points: ECB kept deposit facility rate at 2.00%, main refinancing operations at 2.15%, and marginal lending facility at 2.40%
Annual inflation stood at 2.0% in June 2025, at the ECB's medium-term target; services inflation was 3.3%, goods inflation 0.5%
Economy grew more strongly than expected in Q1 2025 due to frontloaded exports, stronger private consumption and investment
Unemployment stood at 6.3% in May 2025, close to the lowest level since euro introduction
Compensation per employee growth slowed to 3.8% in Q1 2025 from 4.1% in Q4 2024, with unit labour costs also moderating
Most longer-term inflation expectations continue to stand at around 2%
APP and PEPP portfolios declining at measured and predictable pace with no reinvestment of principal payments
Average interest rate on new loans to firms declined to 3.7% in May; mortgage rate stable at 3.3%
why_it_matters: This ECB Economic Bulletin provides the official rationale for holding rates steady at a time when inflation has reached the 2% target. The document signals the ECB is in a data-dependent holding pattern, with particular concern about trade tensions and geopolitical uncertainty. Markets should note that while current conditions support the target, the outlook remains exceptionally uncertain, suggesting the ECB is not pre-committing to future rate moves in either direction.
macro_context: The ECB assesses downside risks to growth from trade tensions, financial market sentiment deterioration, and geopolitical uncertainty (Russia-Ukraine war, Middle East conflict). Upside risks include swift resolution of tensions, higher defence/infrastructure spending, and productivity reforms. For inflation, upside risks include supply chain fragmentation raising import prices, while downside risks include stronger euro, lower export demand, and greater financial market volatility. The document emphasizes the need for fiscal and structural policies to strengthen the euro area economy.
important_numbers: 3.7% average interest rate on new firm loans May 2025
3.3% average interest rate on new mortgages May 2025
2.5% loan growth rate to firms May 2025
3.4% corporate bond issuance growth rate May 2025
notes: Published approximately 8 months before TODAY; classified as historical record. High-quality official ECB publication providing comprehensive assessment of euro area economic conditions and monetary policy rationale. Source is authoritative and directly relevant to macro catalysts topic.
